data.title: U.S. stocks are mixed after weaker-than-expected payrolls, lowering odds of a rate hike
data.description:
Wall Street’s major indexes were mixed on Thursday due to a weaker-than-expected June non-farm payrolls report that lowered expectations for the Federal Reserve to hike interest rates and a tech trade that continues to struggle.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -0.2%, while the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +0.6%, adding to new highs. The heavy-tech Nasdaq (COMP:IND) was last -0.8% as some tech giants such as Tesla (TSLA) and Sandisk (SNDK) declined.

The U.S. economy added 57,000 jobs in June, falling short of the 115,000 increase economists surveyed by Dow Jones had expected. The unemployment rate, however, unexpectedly declined to 4.2%, versus forecasts for it to hold at 4.3%.

Moreover, initial jobless claims fell to 215,000 for the week ended June 27, beating estimates.

Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh said on Wednesday that the Fed will “chart a new course” on forward guidance, contrasting with his predecessor's practice of indicating which way policymakers were leaning in upcoming meetings.

Odds of an interest rate hike before the end of the year fell, while odds for a 25-basis point cut rose, according to the CME FedWatch Tool.

Bond yields were lower. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 2 basis points lower at 4.47%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) dropped 5 basis points to 4.13%.

In addition, the U.S. and Iran are conducting negotiations in Doha that focus on the Strait of Hormuz. The Trump administration is trying to convince Iran that it will gain more from a nuclear deal than from tolls in the strait.

Crude oil futures (CL1:COM) were lower at $67, while Brent (CO1:COM) was $70 per barrel.

data.title: Two Harbors shareholders vote in favor of sale to CrossCountry
data.description:
Two Harbors Investment (TWO) shareholders voted to approve its sale to CrossCountry Mortgage for $12 a share in cash.

Shareholders approved the acquisition at a vote on Thursday, investors who listened to the webcast of the meeting told Seeking Alpha.

Two Harbors didn't immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment on Thursday.

The latest vote comes after Two Harbors (TWO) last Tuesday adjourned its special meeting of holders until today to provide more time to solicit proxies in favor of the acquisition by CrossCountry.

The struggle for control of Two Harbors (TWO) has dragged on since December, when mortgage wholesaler UWM Holdings (UWMC) agreed to acquire Two Harbors (TWO) in a $1.3B all-stock deal. In March, another bidder, CrossCountry, offered an all-cash proposal. Soon after, TWO terminated its deal with UWMC, calling CrossCountry's proposal "superior." Since then, each side has revised its proposal for the mortgage REIT.

Two Harbors edged higher by 0.6% on Thursday.

data.title: Weak job data handed Bitcoin a lifeline: BTC price reclaims $62K; who else helped?
data.description:
Bitcoin (BTC-USD) jumped more than ~4% on Thursday to reclaim the ~$62K level as investors reacted positively to weaker-than-expected U.S. jobs data and falling expectations of another Federal Reserve rate hike.

The U.S. economy added just 57K jobs in June, far below estimates of 114K. May payroll figures were also revised lower by 43K jobs. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate fell to 4.2%, slightly better than expectations of 4.3%.

Following the data, traders sharply reduced their bets on a July rate hike. According to the CME FedWatch tool, futures pricing, and markets now see an 82.4% chance that rates will remain unchanged at the Fed's July meeting.

On the other hand, the probability of a 25-basis-point hike dropped to 17.6%, down from 28.9% a day earlier and 32.1% a week ago.

Walter Bloomberg highlighted in a recent X post that traders in short-term interest rate futures were trimming rate hike bets after the weaker jobs report.

The shift in policy expectations boosted sentiment across risk assets, including cryptocurrencies like Ethereum (ETH-USD), XRP (XRP-USD), Solana (SOL-USD), and Hyperliquid (HYPE). 

Bitcoin price responded strongly, climbing back above the key $60K resistance zone, suggesting buyers are regaining momentum after weeks of range-bound trading. Daily trading volume rose 28% to $44.09B, showing renewed buying interest.

Technical indicators also turned more supportive. A bullish MACD crossover, improving volume, and an RSI near 62 propose momentum currently favors buyers.

However, one area remains weak. Spot Bitcoin ETFs recorded net outflows of $294.62M on July 1, indicating fear despite the recent recovery.

If expectations for higher interest rates continue to fade, the asset could find additional support in the coming weeks, although the pullback from ~$62K to around $61.7K at the time of writing suggests caution remains.

data.title: Pembina Pipeline to advance C$4.6B data center power project in Alberta
data.description:
Pembina Pipeline (PBA) said Thursday it made a positive final investment decision to advance the Greenlight Electricity Center, a 932 MW gas-fired combined cycle power generation facility to be built in Alberta to serve a major data center development.

Greenlight, a partnership owned 47.5% by Pembina (PBA), 47.5% by Morgan Stanley Infrastructure Partners, and 5% by Kineticor Asset Management, has obtained a Class 3 level capital cost estimate of ~C$4B, which rises to C$4.6B when including interest during construction and financing costs, resulting in ~C$2.1B net to Pembina.

The facility will supply electricity to the data center under a long-term tolling agreement, with an anticipated in-service date for the project in H2 2030; ~85% of the project's cost has been secured under fixed-price agreements, including an engineering, procurement, and construction contract with a consortium of Aecon Group (AEGXF) and Técnicas Reunidas, with a turbine supply agreement with Siemens Energy (SMEGF).

Pembina (PBA) said it expects the project to generate annual run-rate adjusted EBITDA of ~C$310M net to the company once operational.

data.title: AI memory, chip stocks fall as sell-off continues amid global peers tumbling
data.description:
Shares of memory and AI-related stocks fell on Thursday after opening in the green following yesterday's sell-off.

Wall Street averages had opened higher initially after June’s weaker-than-expected nonfarm payrolls report tempered expectations for further Federal Reserve rate hikes. However, the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) fell about 0.58%. Meanwhile, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) climbed about 0.04%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) rose about 0.76%. 

Wednesday's tech sell-off had pressured Asian peers, which opened in the red and tumbled during the day. Several AI and chip stocks were also down premarket in the U.S. before opening in the green. South Korea’s Kospi Index slumped about 7.89%. South Korean memory chip giant SK Hynix (HXSCL) plummeted nearly 15%, while peer Samsung Electronics (SSNLF) tumbled around 9% on the Korea Exchange. Earlier this week, the two countries pledged billions in investment in South Korea aimed at growing the country's semiconductors and AI space. Today the two provided details. 

SK Hynix said it would invest 100T won (about $64.38B) to build memory chip ‌and packaging plants as part of a South Korean investment drive, which was announced earlier this week, aimed at semiconductors and AI. Meanwhile, Samsung plans to invest a combined 140T won ($90B) in South Korea's central Chungcheong region, expanding production of display panels, batteries, semiconductors, and chip materials. Reportedly, SK Hynix is removing price caps from its Long-Term Agreements, or LTAs, recently signed with its clients. Analysts suggest that suppliers' negotiating power is being significantly strengthened as the company, along with rival Samsung, extends contract periods.

"Samsung and SK Hynix now make up around half the Kospi’s total weight, up from around just a quarter at the end of last year," Zavier Wong, analyst at eToro, said in a note, according to CNBC. This highlights the companies' huge influence on the country’s benchmark index.

Hong Kong and Chinese tech stocks also saw red. China’s largest contract chipmaker and foundry Semiconductor Manufacturing International tumbled about 10% in Hong Kong, while Hua Hong Grace, another Chinese chip foundry listed in Hong Kong, slumped nearly 13%.

American memory giant Micron Technology (MU) — which competes with Samsung and SK Hynix to provide High-Bandwidth Memory, or HBM chips to Nvidia — fell nearly 4% on Thursday. Sandisk (SNDK) plummeted nearly 10%, while Western Digital (WDC) and Seagate Technology (STX) each tumbled around 6%.

Shares of AI chipmaker Nvidia (NVDA) dipped around 1%, while Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) slumped nearly 4%. Qualcomm (QCOM) fell about 3%, while Broadcom (AVGO) declined around 1%. Cerebras (CBRS) fell about 5%.

Other AI and networking-related stocks also tumbled on Thursday. Applied Optoelectronics (AAOI) plummeted about 10%, while Corning (GLW) and Coherent (COHR) each slumped nearly 7%.  Lumentum (LITE) and Ciena (CIEN) each tumbled around 6%, while Celestica (CLS) fell nearly 5%. Cisco (CSCO) declined about 3%, while Arista Networks (ANET) dipped nearly 2%.

Shares of Marvell Technology (MRVL) slumped about 8%, while GlobalFoundries (GFS) and Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC) each tumbled nearly 7%. Arm (ARM) fell about 4%, while Intel (INTC) fell nearly 3% despite positive news. HSBC raised Intel's price target to a street-high of $200 from $100, while keeping its Buy rating. Meanwhile, Wedbush noted that Tesla's hiring of Gary Jiang, an 18-year veteran of Intel, to help with its Terafab project is considered a potential “positive” for the chip giant.

Analog Devices (ADI) fell about 2%, while Texas Instruments (TXN) dipped nearly 1%. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) was also in the red.

Chip equipment makers: KLA (KLAC) slumped about 9%, while Lam Research (LRCX) and Applied Materials (AMAT) each declined nearly 8%. ASML (ASML) fell about 3% on Thursday.

Update: The story was updated with stock price movement.

data.title: CoreWeave, Nebius in spotlight as BNP discusses capacity vs. sustainability debate
data.description:
CoreWeave (CRWV) and Nebius (NBIS) were in the spotlight on Thursday as investment firm BNP Paribas weighed in on the debate of providing compute capacity versus long-term sustainability.

“Near term, we continue to find the pricing environment supportive, evidenced in part by SpaceX’s (SPCX) recent AI infrastructure deals with Anthropic and Google, which we estimate generates $30 – 50bn of revenue/GW (it’s unclear where the $6bn Reflection AI deal lands on a revenue/GW basis for SpaceX),” BNP analyst Stefan Slowinski wrote in a note to clients. “While we believe SpaceX’s economics are at least partially driven by the company’s ability to deliver near-term compute to two arguably price-insensitive customers, we still view the deals as indicative of a positive pricing backdrop.”

Delving deeper, Slowinski said the concern around the shift to token optimization from so-called “toxxenmaxxing” is likely to help Nebius, even as demand is still “well ahead of supply.” (Slowinski pointed to the fact that Amazon Web Services (AMZN) recently announced 20% price increases for its GPU reservation service.)

“In this regard, Nebius screens relatively well as its AI cloud stack appears better positioned to support fine-tuned open-source models for enterprise customers seeking cheaper alternatives to frontier models (and frontier lab pricing), adding yet another leg to the stock’s broader narrative (even if this has yet to manifest into reported results),” Slowinski added.

For the larger neocloud sector, Slowinski said the group can accept lower unlevered returns when compared to the hyperscalers given their stacks are still early in their monetization and their financial leverage can enable “attractive project-level levered IRRs.”

Concerning CoreWeave's share price underperformance compared to Nebius, Slowinski said he sees a “potential catch-up opportunity” as execution continued to improve in the second half of 2026.

“The company is adding significant capacity, which masks the underlying unit economics of the business, while newer contracts are being priced to offset component inflation while maintaining mid-20% contribution margins,” Slowinski explained. “Moreover, CoreWeave is simply further along in its capacity build relative to its neocloud peers (~1 GW of active power vs. ~200 MW at Nebius) and could soon reach a point where margin volatility moderates as net new deployments become a smaller percentage of the existing installed base. With a favorable pricing backdrop, effective use of asset-backed leverage, and a broadly declining cost of capital, we continue to view the risk/reward as attractive at these levels.”

Slowinski is still “fundamentally positive” on Nebius, but given the sharp run in the stock this year, he has a Neutral rating on its shares.

data.title: Goldman Sachs raises airlines' outlook, downgrades SkyWest on block hour guidance
data.description:
Goldman Sachs is raising its outlook on the airline industry as strong demand for air travel has yet to be blunted by increased fares to compensate for higher fuel prices.

Accordingly, the firm raised its Q3 and Q4 2026 industry net income forecast by 24% and 32%, respectively.

“We believe revenue trends have been bolstered by the industry’s quick response to moderate supply and the improvement in the competitive environment following Spirit’s cessation of service in May,” Goldman analyst Catherine O’Brien said in her note to clients.

O’Brien singles out Allegiant Travel Company (ALGT) for its post-Sun Country merger outlook, noting the combined company’s “significantly improved revenue trends.”

“We expect strong revenue trends at Allegiant to continue as it benefits from lower leisure-focused capacity and ramping merger synergies,” O’Brien says, reiterating her Buy rating on the carrier and raising Allegiant’s (ALGT) price target by 14% to $142.

O’Brien also raised the target price for Alaska Air (ALK) by 19% to $69, American Airlines (AAL) by 50% to $15, Delta (DAL) by 45% to $116, Sell-rated JetBlue (JBLU) by 28% to $4.50, Southwest (LUV) by 17%, and United (UAL) by 24% to $162.

The outlier is SkyWest (SKYW), which O’Brien downgraded to Neutral from Buy with a 14% reduction to its price target (now at $108) on increased downside risk to block hour production.

SkyWest’s (SKYW) business is primarily driven by long-term capacity purchase agreements with its partner airlines, measured as block hours. During the first quarter earnings call, management indicated it expects block hour production will be “slightly” lower this summer than modeled for last quarter. 

This led O’Brien to caution that while fuel prices have come down materially from the April peak, “they remain elevated vs. pre-war levels, and we therefore expect industry capacity growth to remain lower than initially expected over the medium term, increasing downside risk to SkyWest’s block hour production.”

O’Brien lowered her 2026 block hour growth forecast to 3.0% from 3.5%, previously, and from 4.9% earlier this year, representing a “significant deceleration” from recent years.  

SkyWest (SKYW) shares are lower on Thursday, currently challenging support at the 200-day moving average.

data.title: Anthropic considers building in-house AI chip with Samsung as key partner: report
data.description:
Anthropic (ANTHRO) is considering building an in-house AI chip and is in discussions with Samsung (SSNLF) as a potential manufacturing partner in the project, according to The Information.

If the potential deal becomes finalized, Anthropic would use Samsung's foundry business and 2-nanometer technology, the report said, citing sources with knowledge on the matter.

In recent years, several big tech companies outside the semiconductor sector have turned to developing in-house, custom silicon. Google (GOOG)(GOOGL), Microsoft (MSFT) and Amazon (AMZN) all build lines of in-house AI chips.

Last week, Anthropic's chief rival, OpenAI (OPENAI), revealed Jalapeño, a new chip designed specifically to run inference for large language models. They partnered with Broadcom (AVGO) and Celestica (CLS) on the project.

data.title: AI data centers are reshaping the energy infrastructure landscape
data.description:
Investors Should Know: AI data center expansion is driving fresh demand for power and energy infrastructure, putting a diverse group of utilities and energy suppliers in focus for investors tracking the buildout.

Background

The rapid growth of AI data centers has created a surge in electricity demand that existing grid infrastructure was not built to handle. Power-hungry computing facilities require reliable, large-scale energy supply — and that need is pulling capital toward a broad range of energy providers.

The suppliers positioned to meet this demand span multiple energy types: fuel cell technology, nuclear power, natural gas generation, and traditional electric utilities. Each offers a different risk and return profile, but all share exposure to the same underlying driver — the need for more power, delivered reliably, at scale.

Stocks to Watch

Bloom Energy Corporation (BE) is classified as a core AI power supplier, with a year-to-date return of 248% reflecting strong market interest in its fuel cell technology. Talen Energy Corporation (TLN) and Oklo Inc. (OKLO) represent the nuclear side of the equation, with Talen focused on operating generation assets and Oklo developing next-generation small modular reactors.

American Electric Power Company (AEP), one of the largest U.S. electric utilities by market cap at roughly $74 billion, also carries the AI power supplier classification. The AES Corporation (AES), TransAlta Corporation (TAC), Capital Power Corp. (CPXWF), Fermi Inc. (FRMI), Capstone Energy+, Inc. (CGEH), and Power Solutions International (PSIX) round out the thematic universe.

AI Power Infrastructure in Focus

data.title: SEC looks into alleged insider trades on China's brokerage crackdown - report
data.description:
The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission is investigating claims made by Susquehanna International Group that unidentified insider traders reaped $100M on option bets placed before Chinese authorities cracked down on cross-border brokerages, according to a media report.

Susquehanna made the claims in a lawsuit filed Monday. The SEC is now looking at the trades described in the market-making company's complaint, Bloomberg News reported late Wednesday, citing a person familiar with the matter.

In the suit against 100 John Doe defendants, Susquehanna said it lost more than $70M as a counterparty on most of the alleged insider trades. The firm said it doesn't know who made the trades but said "the high-risk, high-reward" option bets could only be plausibly explained as insider trading.

On Monday, a federal judge granted Susquehanna's request to freeze accounts at Interactive Brokers (IBKR), Futu Holdings (FUTU), and Up Fintech Holdings (TIGR), platforms that the defendants allegedly used to make the trades. 

Up Fintech's (TIGR) Tiger Brokers and  Futu (FUTU) were also subjects of the Chinese government's investigation into offshore brokerages operating unlicensed trading services for mainland China residents.

A spokesperson for Interactive Brokers (IBKR) told Bloomberg it's cooperating with Susquehanna and will cooperate with relevant regulators.

Interactive Brokers (IBKR) stock rose 0.6% in Thursday morning trading. Futu (FUTU) fell 0.7%, and UP Fintech (TIGR) slipped 0.7%.
